_meta.purpose: Floating microdrama concept registry for Dear.AI. /microdramaideas pulls from this. Edit freely — add new ideas, mark status, archive ones we've dropped.

ideas.tagline_en: A modern US Army Ranger gets stuck in a medieval magical kingdom, accidentally kills the slave-trading family that took him in, has to fake being a magical prodigy to hold the estate, and is being heckled by the ghosts of everyone he killed while falling for the survivor who watched him do it.
ideas.genre: power fantasy · transmigration · comedy · LitRPG · steampunk-fantasy
ideas.tonal_register: Sakamoto Days × Hitman Reborn × One Punch Man × Connecticut Yankee × Cradle. Punisher demeanor (gruff, deadpan, traumatized vet) — NOT Punisher mission.
ideas.lead: Cole Walker — 36, US Army Ranger, 3rd tour Afghanistan, lost a pregnancy two weeks before deployment, transmigrated via artillery shell.
ideas.setting: Medieval magical kingdom (unnamed; pending) + Vaehn Manor as primary repeat-set. Earth-side parallel via Sarah Walker (wife) in Maryland.
ideas.dopamine_engine: Fake-magic engine — each ep Cole 'casts' one Earth-tech 'spell' (Tesla coil, fishing line, radio earpiece) the audience knows is fake but in-world characters believe is real. Ghost ensemble (7 Vaehns) heckle + advise. Forge Episodes every 5-10 eps reveal new gadgets. Steampunk visual signature.

Mira Vaehn (indentured archivist, love interest, co-conspirator)
Sarah Walker (Earth wife, arrives in S1 finale)

Jesha Vaehn (ghost #6, Mira's complicated cousin)
Old Aunt Yula (ghost #7, senile, crosses over peacefully in finale)
Princess Veyl (Crown reconnaissance, falls for Cole anyway)
King Aurelius IV (final-act antagonist, champion-mage)
Brin (half-elven research assistant, comic relief)
Tash (manor steward, Cole's de-facto chief of staff)
Old Hayden (senile living tutor, pairs with Yula for cross-realm comedy)
ideas.why_team: Cleanest US 60-ep play on the slate. Episodic + arc structure means writers' room can build forward without locking full season arc up front. Western LitRPG audience (Cradle / DCC / HWFWM / Iron Druid) is the largest paying genre demo Dear.AI can reach right now. Fake-magic engine generates infinite cliffs. Ghost ensemble = unique comedy + emotional ensemble. AI-native steampunk visual signature impossible at practical budget. Score 35/35 on 60-ep legs test.
ideas.scale: 60 eps Season 1 · multi-season ceiling (S2 hook = US military arrives via portal)
ideas.title_evolution: DRY COUNTRY (Wild West, archived) → workshop (VAEHN / ZERO MAGE / WRONG MAGUS / PRETENDER) → LORD WALKER (briefly locked 2026-05-20 morning) → FALSE MAGUS (final, 2026-05-20 evening). Cole's surname Walker is unchanged as character name.
ideas.notes: Concept evolved from DRY COUNTRY (Wild West 1882, archived). Title locked 2026-05-20 evening as FALSE MAGUS (after brief LORD WALKER lock earlier same day). Cole's surname Walker retained as character name. Next milestone: Ep 1-6 scripted-level outlines. Open questions documented in show bible.

ideas.tagline_en: A Jakarta housemaid witnesses her American expat boss murder his business partner — and discovers everyone she could turn to is already on his payroll.
ideas.tagline_id: Seorang pembantu di Jakarta menyaksikan majikan ekspatriat Amerika-nya membunuh mitra bisnisnya — dan menyadari semua orang yang bisa dia hubungi sudah dibayar oleh majikannya.
ideas.genre: class thriller · Parasite × I Care a Lot × Get Out
ideas.lead: Sari Wulan — 26, Javanese domestic worker, 4 years employed by the Hendersons
ideas.dopamine_engine: Audience roots for Sari from frame 1. Cleanest underdog setup possible. Forced isolation (no police, no embassy, no home). Coalition-building one ally per ep. Every ep ends mid-escape, mid-betrayal, or mid-reveal.

ideas.why_team: Universally legible class thriller. Lean cast, contained Jakarta locations. Festival-friendly AND microdrama-friendly. Sari role is a career-launcher. Best as 10-ep prestige limited series, not 60-ep microdrama.

ideas.status: concept locked 2026-05-21 · Singapore Chinese-family build · WhatsApp-family-group cold open (daughter + wife co-POV) · Ep 1 atomized v2 · awaiting full season arc
ideas.tagline_en: Aunty Doris saw the clip first. Forwarded it to the family WhatsApp group with one line: 'Aiyooo Ken still working there right?' Sophia saw it at her polyclinic counter. She didn't reply. Fourteen relatives watched her not reply. By the time Megan called her mother from school, the extended family already knew her father had been retrenched. Sophia still didn't.
ideas.genre: AI-era family microdrama · Singapore Chinese family · social-realist thriller
ideas.tonal_register: Anthony Chen × Royston Tan × Hirokazu Kore-eda. Documentary-tier Singapore Chinese family realism. The family is GOOD — we want them to make it. The villain is off-screen and faceless (a CEO in Hong Kong, an algorithm in a server farm, a system). Microdrama-paced (90-120s episodes, 60 eps S1) but festival-grade family scenes underneath.
ideas.lead_family: THE TAN FAMILY of Bishan, Singapore — 5-room HDB, 8 years into a 25-year mortgage, paid-off Honda Stream in the carpark.
ideas.lead: Kenneth Tan — 43, Chinese-Singaporean, 17 years at Standard Chartered Singapore back-office ops (anonymizable to 'Crown Bank Singapore' if legal requires). The kind of dad who walks his daughter to the MRT every morning. Wife packs his Tupperware lunch with siu mai. Goes to Bishan North CC for badminton on Saturday mornings. Mother in Tiong Bahru, calls every Sunday. The good son. The dependable husband. The dad who has never missed a Sunday dim sum in fifteen years.
ideas.pov_lead: MEGAN TAN — 15, Sec 3 at a top-tier school (Raffles Girls' or similar tier). The eyes of the show. She is the one who sees first. The audience experiences the world through her until Ep 21.
ideas.setting: Singapore · contemporary (May 2026 onwards) · primary 5-room HDB in Bishan + Crown Bank Singapore office tower at Marina Bay + Megan's Sec 3 classroom + Sophia's polyclinic + the Tiong Bahru flat where Kenneth's mother lives + a Bukit Merah industrial-park data-labelling office (Act 2-3)
ideas.real_world_anchor: Bill Winters, Standard Chartered CEO, May 19 2026 Hong Kong investor day. Announced 7,000+ back-office cuts by 2030 across Chennai · Bengaluru · KL · Warsaw. (For SG-set version: dramatic license to include Singapore as a fifth named city. Anonymize the bank if needed.) The on-record quote: 'We're replacing lower-value human capital with the investment capital we're putting in.' Source: techtimes.com/articles/316851/20260519/standard-chartered-cut-7000-back-office-jobs-2030-first-major-bank-put-ai-layoff-schedule.htm
ideas.dopamine_engine: TEN-MINUTE LOAD: The family is so achingly nice that the audience loves them by the end of the cold open. Kenneth's kaya-toast breakfast, Sophia packing the Tupperware, Megan walking with her dad to the MRT, Ryan (the 10-year-old son) folding paper aeroplanes with him on Sunday. Then the gut punch. Then forced isolation: Kenneth cannot tell Sophia (she'd worry), cannot tell po-po (his mother in Tiong Bahru would die before she let him be ashamed), cannot tell Megan (who already knows). Three-POV engine: Megan (the witness, Eps 1-20), Sophia (the discoverer, Eps 21-40), Kenneth (the survivor, Eps 41-60). Each act flips who is hiding from whom.

Sophia Tan (wife · 40 · senior administrator at a polyclinic · the steady salary · doesn't know yet)

Cheryl Goh (labour-rights lawyer at TWC2 · 36 · the only one who tells him the truth · can only do so much)
Daniel Cheng (CNA Insider reporter · 33 · writing 'the larger story' on AI displacement in SG banking · uses Kenneth without protecting him)
Adrian Wong (the man who runs the Bukit Merah data-labelling office · 29 · the system's foot soldier · not a villain, just younger and more pragmatic)
Aunty Doris (Sophia's elder sister · 52 · the family WhatsApp group's most-active forwarder · the unintentional fuse-lighter in Ep 1 · returns as recurring well-meaning antagonist throughout S1)
Uncle Chee Wee (Sophia's brother-in-law · 56 · senior civil servant · the family's voice of 'I told you so')
Cousin Jolene (Megan's cousin · 19 · NUS first year · TikTok-fluent · the first family member to research what 'lower-value human capital' actually means)
BILL WINTERS (real-name avatar · seen only on TV / phone screens · the off-screen god of the show · spoken about but never met)
ideas.cold_open_locked: BLACK SCREEN. WhatsApp notification PING. Then another. Then another. Then a flurry. Phone screen reveals: 'Tan Family ❤️' group chat. New message thumbnail: Bill Winters podium video (intentionally pixellated thumbnail). Caption from AUNTY DORIS: 'Aiyoooo see this lah 😱'. Cascade of replies, fast: AUNTY DORIS: 'Ken still working there right?' / UNCLE CHEE WEE: 'Sophia 😨' / COUSIN JOLENE: 'Wah no way.' / AUNTIE BETH: 'Don't panic don't panic let's call her first'. Then: 'Sophia is typing...' blinking. Long beat. STOPS. Cut to SOPHIA at the polyclinic counter, frozen, phone in hand. TITLE CARD: LAID OFF BY AI · EP 1.
ideas.ep1_cold_open_rationale: Earlier draft had Megan watching the clip in a Sec 3 social studies class. User flagged 2026-05-21: SG schools would not show that clip (politically sensitive, parents at the bank, principal calls by 4pm). Pivoted to the WhatsApp family-group mechanism — uniquely Singaporean delivery vector. The horror underneath: in Singapore, your aunty knows you've been retrenched before your wife does, because she's in 14 group chats and yours isn't one of them. The 'Sophia is typing...' that stops is the silent-scream beat. The audience hears Winters' actual voice for the first time at beat 10 (Megan scrolls up to find the clip in her earphones at school) — earning the quote rather than weaponizing it at frame 1.
ep1_atomized_beats: 2. Phone screen: 'Tan Family ❤️' group. Aunty Doris's forwarded Bill Winters clip thumbnail + caption 'Aiyoooo see this lah 😱' (3-8s)
3. Cascade of family replies (Aunty Doris, Uncle Chee Wee, Cousin Jolene, Auntie Beth) — fast, panicked, Singlish-textured (8-18s)
4. 'Sophia is typing...' blinking. Long beat. STOPS. (18-23s)
5. TITLE CARD: LAID OFF BY AI · EP 1 (23-25s)
6. SOPHIA at the polyclinic counter, holding her phone, frozen. A patient is at her counter saying her name. She doesn't hear. (25-35s)
7. Sophia walks to the staff toilet, locks the door. Calls Kenneth. The call connects and rings. (35-45s)
8. KENNETH in a Crown Bank toilet at Marina Bay. Just been told. Tie undone. Phone face-down on the sink, vibrating. He doesn't pick up. (45-55s)
9. MEGAN at school, lunch break, canteen. Her phone is buzzing. Aunty Doris is PMing her directly: 'Ah girl, mummy okay or not? Cannot reach her 😟' (55-65s)
10. Megan opens the family group, scrolls up, watches the Winters clip through her earphones. AUDIENCE HEARS THE QUOTE FOR THE FIRST TIME — Winters' actual voice. (65-75s)
11. Megan looks up. Around her, classmates eating chicken rice, laughing. She is alone in the noise. (75-80s)
12. Megan stands. Picks up her tray. Walks out. (80-85s)
13. School carpark. Megan calls Dad. Voicemail. Calls Sophia. Sophia picks up on the first ring. Megan: 'Mummy. Aunty Doris messaged me. Where's daddy.' (85-92s)
14. CLIFF: Long silence on the line. Then SOPHIA, quiet: 'Ah girl. I don't know.' CUT TO BLACK. EP 1 END. (92-98s)
ideas.ep1_runtime_target: 90-98 seconds. Vertical 9:16. ~28-32 atomic shots at 3-4s each. Three character-specific locations total: polyclinic counter + staff toilet (Sophia, ~10 shots), Crown Bank toilet (Kenneth, ~6 shots), school canteen + carpark (Megan, ~12 shots), plus phone-screen close-ups (~6 shots). No classroom set required. No extras with speaking parts in Ep 1.
ideas.season_arc_sketch: S1 = 60 eps. Three acts of 20. ACT 1 (Eps 1-20 · Megan's POV): The family hides from itself. Kenneth pretends to go to work every morning. Sophia pretends to believe him. Megan watches everything. Po-po (Mdm Tan) calls every Sunday — they all lie. Ep 20 cliff: Po-po sees Kenneth at a hawker centre in the middle of a weekday. ACT 2 (Eps 21-40 · Sophia's POV): The marriage cracks. Sophia takes on a second shift at the polyclinic. Their CPF nest egg starts evaporating. Kenneth applies for 47 jobs. Gets zero callbacks. Takes the Bukit Merah data-labelling gig. Sophia finds the paystub. The fight. Ep 40 cliff: Sophia files for separation papers — to protect Kenneth's CPF from creditors. He doesn't understand at first; thinks she's leaving him. ACT 3 (Eps 41-60 · Kenneth's POV): At the data-labelling office, Kenneth recognises the data he's labelling — it's redacted versions of his own past work files from Crown Bank. He's training his replacement. The thriller turn: sabotage (lose the gig, lose the family) or feed it cleanly (accelerate the system that destroyed him)? Finale: he chooses to feed it cleanly — but quietly poisons one record per shift. A long con. S1 ends with Kenneth still labelling. The system still winning. But the records are dirty. And the family is, for the first time in eighteen months, eating dim sum together on a Sunday.
ideas.why_team: Singapore-set + Chinese-family build = highest direct audience identification for Raymus's home market. The 'nice family' load is the engine — every minute Megan, Ryan, and Sophia spend on screen makes the gut punch hurt more. Daughter-POV cold open is the cleanest microdrama hook on the slate — audience is inside the wound before the title card. AI-era story is timely (Bill Winters real-world quote does 80% of audience-anger pre-loading for free). Production-friendly: 5-6 contained locations · 5 core characters · no wide ensemble · classroom + HDB interior + MRT are all AI-pipeline-locked location refs already. Tonal register (Anthony Chen × Royston Tan × Kore-eda) gives festival edge — Eps 1-6 could submit to Singapore Short Film Awards, Eps 11-30 could pitch to Mediacorp / Viu Originals / Netflix SG as long-form.
ideas.scale: 60 eps S1 · vertical 9:16 microdrama · multi-season ceiling (S2 = the AI starts failing on real customer cases, regulators investigate, Kenneth is the one person who knows why)
open_questions: Bank name — use real Standard Chartered SG (legal risk · maximum punch · the show becomes about a real institution) or anonymized Crown Bank Singapore (safer · still legible · most viewers will know what we mean)?
Bill Winters appearance — real CEO likeness on smartboard / TV screens (legal risk · documentary punch) or composite CEO (safer · slightly less punchy · loses the 'this really happened' charge)?
Ep 1 daughter walking out of class — does the school PUNISH her later in the season for it? Could be an Act 2 subplot (the school summons Sophia, who hasn't been told yet).
Po-po's role — does she find out in Ep 20 and CARRY the family from there, or does Kenneth keep hiding it from her for the full 60? The second is more painful and more Singaporean.
Ryan (10-year-old son) — when does HE find out? Currently slotted to be the heart that breaks last (around Ep 50). Should it be earlier?

Ending tone — Kenneth 'wins' silently (long-con poisoning ends S1 on quiet defiance) OR Kenneth loses cleanly (gets caught poisoning the data, fired again, but the family eats dim sum on Sunday for the first time in 18 months)? The second is the better ending if we want a multi-season show.

Language distribution — primary English (top-tier school for Megan, professional workplace for Kenneth) with Mandarin at po-po's flat, Hokkien at hawker centres, and Singlish throughout? Confirm house policy.
Verbatim quote on screen — Bill Winters' real words attributed verbatim, or paraphrased? Verbatim is the show's spine; legal review required.
ideas.notes: Concept relocated 2026-05-21 (evening session) from KL Tamil-Malaysian male protagonist to Singapore Chinese family. POV pivoted from father to daughter for the cold open — daughter-in-classroom-watching-her-dad-be-called-'lower-value' is the strongest gut punch on the slate. Family unit (Kenneth · Sophia · Megan · Ryan · Po-po) is intentionally written as the most achingly nice Singaporean Chinese family possible — the dim sum Sundays, the Bishan CC badminton, the Tiong Bahru po-po — because every minute of niceness compounds the wound. Next milestones: lock anonymization decision, draft Ep 1 full script in v2.2 shotlist format, talent-pool for the four leads (43 Chinese-Singaporean tired-but-dignified dad · 40 polyclinic admin mum · 15 Sec 3 daughter · 10 Pri 4 son · 71 po-po), location lock for Bishan HDB interior, Marina Bay tower exterior, and Sec 3 classroom set.

ideas.tagline_en: The same anxieties, thirty years apart. A Singaporean mother sat PSLE in 1996. Her son sits PSLE in 2026. Same HDB. Same exam. Same panic. Different aesthetics. Two parallel timelines that converge in one silent gesture in a car.
ideas.genre: satire · generational drama · short film · period-mirror
ideas.tonal_register: Edward Yang × Anthony Chen × Wong Kar-wai (period work). Quiet. Observational. Almost no dialogue for the first 4 minutes — pure parallel cutting between 1996 and 2026.
ideas.lead: MOTHER (40 in 2026, 11 in 1996) — same character, two ages, played by two actresses with matching face refs. SON (11, 2026) — present-day timeline.
ideas.setting: A single Bishan HDB block and its surrounding void deck, in two time periods. 1996 dressed (pastel HDB facade, Walkman, MRT in original livery, paper exam booklets, ceiling fans, school uniforms in 90s cut). 2026 contemporary (climate-controlled exam hall, iPad-prep, AL band scoring, smart parking, same HDB block).
ideas.engine: Pure parallel cutting. 4 minutes of no dialogue. Same anxieties: pre-exam panic, blank window stare, walk home alone, mother's gaze. The ENDING turn breaks the silence — present-day mother picks her son up from PSLE, opens her mouth to ask 'how was it,' STOPS herself, cuts to 1996 mum at 11 getting picked up by HER mother — *'How was it?'* — 'don't ask me' — mum-1996 gets slapped in the car. Present-day mum reaches over and puts her hand on her son's hand instead. Last shot: he looks at her like he's never seen her before.
key_cast: Mother-2026 (40 · the present-day mother · post-PSLE collection)
Mother-1996 (11 · the same character as a child · sitting PSLE herself)
Son-2026 (11 · the present-day son · sitting PSLE in the contemporary timeline)
Grandmother-1996 (38 · the 1996 mother of Mother-1996 · the one who slaps)
1996 classroom extras (faces seen, no lines)
2026 exam hall extras (faces seen, no lines)
ideas.real_world_anchor: Singapore PSLE generational trauma. 1996 was peak Singapore tiger-mum era; 2026 is the AL-banded version of the same pressure cooker. Documented by CNA Insider features 2022-2024 on intergenerational PSLE anxiety.
ideas.ai_production_fit: STRONG. Period work (1996) is AI's sweet spot — set-dress costumes via character refs, no need to source vintage uniforms or build period sets. Parallel cutting between two timelines uses identical Bishan HDB location with different art-direction passes. Character refs lock identity across two actresses playing the same person at two ages. Voice work minimal (almost no dialogue). Soundtrack drives the trailer beat — a recognizable 90s Mandopop track scored against the 1996 sequences, dropped to silence for the 2026 cuts.
ideas.scale: 4-6 min short film · single deliverable · festival qualifying (SG Short Film Awards) · IGTV / YouTube / LinkedIn long-form crossover
ideas.product_placement: Incidental only. Could appear on the dashboard of the car the present-day mother drives, or as the app icon on the son's iPad. Brand pays for cultural adjacency. NO CTA card.
open_questions: Lead casting — same actress as 11-year-old + adult, or two different actresses with matching face refs?
1996 dialect — Mandarin-heavy or Hokkien-heavy? (1996 SG households trended Hokkien at home for working-class Chinese.)
Slap moment — show on screen or imply via car door slam + cut? Tonal call.
Final shot duration — long hold on son's face (Kore-eda), or cut to black on contact?
Soundtrack — licensed 90s Mandopop (legal) or original score in 90s palette (free, less recognizable)?
ideas.why_team: Distinctively Raymus. Period work is your signature. No SG ad agency is touching generational PSLE trauma at this craft level. The Jack Neo lane is comedy; the prestige short-film lane is wide open. Soundtrack potential is enormous. Festival-qualifying. Festival qualifying + brand-friendly (incidental product placement only).
ideas.notes: Proposed 2026-05-21 in PSLE-satire ideation session. User signaled brand-incidental positioning. This is the strongest short-film concept on the slate. Next milestones: lock casting question (same actress vs two), lock soundtrack legality question, draft 1-page treatment + storyboard pass.

ideas.tagline_en: A church basement. Folding chairs in a circle. A nervous mother stands up: 'Hi, my name is Janice and I have a Primary 5 son.' Group: 'Hi Janice.' She confesses to having checked his MarkMyAssignment app seven hours ago. Polite applause from the room. The satire holds for sixty seconds — until Mira walks in and says her daughter doesn't want to come home from school anymore.
ideas.genre: satire · social comedy · short film · satire-to-real pivot
ideas.tonal_register: Christopher Guest mockumentary × Anthony Chen × the AA-meeting scene in Anonymous Alcoholics films. Single location. Group of 6-7 parents in a circle. Polite applause. Calm yoga-teacher moderator. Slow comedic build, then a hard pivot to real emotion in the last minute.
ideas.lead: JANICE TAN — 41 · Type A Singaporean mother · the cold-open confessor. Joined by 5-6 other parents (mix of mothers and fathers). MIRA — 38 · the pivot character who walks in late in the film and breaks the satire frame.
ideas.setting: A community-hall room or church basement · Singapore · folding chairs in a circle · tea urn in the back · ceiling fan · fluorescent lighting · entire film in one room
ideas.engine: Satire to real. Single location. 5-6 PSLE parents take turns confessing increasingly absurd things: 'I bought four assessment books last Saturday. My husband doesn't know about the fourth one.' / 'I joined the parent WhatsApp under a fake name to spy on what the top mum is saying.' / 'I refresh the school portal every 15 minutes between 2 and 4am.' Polite group murmurs. Moderator leads them in the recovery prayer: 'Grant me the serenity to accept the AL band I cannot change...' Then MIRA walks in. Doesn't crack a joke. 'I came because my daughter told me she didn't want to come home from school anymore. She didn't say it crying. She said it like a fact. She's eleven.' Room goes silent. The leader nods slowly: 'Yeah. That's why most of us are here too.'

ideas.tagline_en: Aunty caught him at the stairwell at 11:15pm. Phone in his hand, plugged into the lift-lobby socket. She didn't ask what he was doing — she called his mother. Mother threw the phone in the bin. He stopped talking for three days. Two weeks later, the school called her in. Her son was number 2 in the cohort. She didn't understand. She thought the school had made a mistake.
ideas.genre: AI access-flip drama · social-realist · SG underclass · child POV
ideas.tonal_register: Anthony Chen Ilo Ilo × Hirokazu Kore-eda × Ramin Bahrani Chop Shop. Quiet. Observational. Child POV. The HDB void deck is a character. The film is mostly silent — the kid doesn't talk much, mother doesn't talk much, the AI on the phone screen does most of the explaining.
ideas.lead: WEI JIE — 11, Primary 5 · Chinese-Singaporean · lives in a 1-room rental flat in Bukit Merah with mother (single, hospital cleaner), baby sister (8 months), and grandmother (74, mid-stage dementia)
ideas.setting: Bukit Merah 1-room rental block · 1972 construction · pastel-painted walls · void deck stairwell · lift lobby with the wall socket · the boy's primary school 3 MRT stops away · the school principal's office in Act 2
ideas.real_world_anchor: Singapore rental-flat households (1-room and 2-room flats) are ~5% of SG resident households (HDB data), concentrated in mature estates including Bukit Merah, Toa Payoh, Lengkok Bahru, Chai Chee, Geylang. Smartphone penetration in these households is universal (handed-down devices). Free Wi-Fi at the void deck via Wireless@SG (national program). Free-tier AI access (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini) is real and available. The MOE has been cautious about classroom AI but does not block personal use. The access-flip thesis is real and underreported in SG media.
ideas.engine_short_film: Single-day timeline + flashback structure. COLD OPEN: 11:15pm, aunty catches Wei Jie in the stairwell, calls mother. Mother arrives shamed and panicked. Throws phone in bin. ARC: 3 days of silence. Mother thinks he was watching porn / chatting with strangers / running away. Then the school summons her. Reveal in the principal's office — Wei Jie is number 2 in the cohort. Mother does not understand. School pulls up his usage history. The teacher has noticed: 'his work has been improving for 8 months.' Mother goes home. Looks in the bin. The phone is still there. CLOSING SHOT: she sits with him on the stairwell. Doesn't speak. Hands him the phone. He looks at her. Plugs it in.
ideas.engine_series_version: 20-ep microdrama. ACT 1 (Eps 1-7): The stairwell life — Wei Jie discovers AI, climbs from bottom to top quartile, hides everything. Ep 7 cliff: aunty catches him. ACT 2 (Eps 8-14): Mother's POV — the discovery, the silence, the school summons, the bin. Ep 14 cliff: mother hands him the phone back. ACT 3 (Eps 15-20): The school doesn't believe him — accusation of cheating, formal disciplinary process. He has to demonstrate his learning to a panel. Mother shows up. Finale: he stays in school. Pri 6 begins. PSLE looming. Mother starts using the AI too — to learn English so she can read the school letters.

ideas.tagline_en: Every night at 8:55pm, before the Bukit Merah Public Library closes, the night librarian and a 13-year-old kid drink tea together. The librarian is finishing his Masters in education. The kid has nowhere else to go. They are each other's classroom. On the last Friday of October, the library tells Ryan his contract is not being renewed — they are installing an AI chatbot at the front desk to replace overnight staff. The same AI that has been teaching Jia En to read.
ideas.genre: AI access-flip drama · social-realist · two-character intimacy · Singapore
ideas.tonal_register: Hirokazu Kore-eda × Wong Kar-wai × Anthony Chen. Two-character. Single location. Intimate. The library at night is a character. Long takes. Natural light. Minimal dialogue. The film says everything by withholding.
ideas.leads: JIA EN — 13 · Sec 1 · Chinese-Singaporean · lives with his mother (working two jobs) in a 3-room HDB; grandmother in palliative care at Bright Vision. RYAN — 28 · Malay-Singaporean · part-time night librarian at Bukit Merah Public Library · finishing his Masters in education (NIE) · believes deeply in libraries as the last free institution.
ideas.setting: Bukit Merah Public Library · evening to 9pm close · plus the lift lobby + Jia En's HDB block exterior + Bright Vision corridor (for grandmother scenes) · contemporary Singapore
ideas.real_world_anchor: NLB libraries across Singapore are currently exploring AI chatbots for reference and circulation desk replacement (real, ongoing 2025-2026). Public libraries have always been the institution of last resort for kids with nowhere to study at home — documented in NLB community impact reports. The night-librarian role is real and threatened. The two are about to collide.
ideas.engine_short_film: 10-12 minute single-location two-character film. STRUCTURE: Three Fridays in October. Friday 1 — Ryan notices Jia En is still in the reading room at 8:50pm. They have tea. Jia En teaches Ryan a Hokkien phrase his grandmother used to say. Friday 2 — They've been having tea every night for a month. Jia En shows Ryan how to ask Claude better questions; Ryan teaches Jia En how to fact-check what Claude says. They are teaching each other. Friday 3 — Ryan tells Jia En his contract isn't being renewed. The library is installing a chatbot at the front desk. He's the last night librarian. Jia En asks: 'Where will I go?' Ryan doesn't have an answer. FINAL SHOT: Jia En at the void deck of his HDB block, with his phone, alone at 9:30pm. He opens Claude. Types: 'Hi.' Cuts to black.
ideas.engine_series_version: 20-ep limited series. ACT 1 (Eps 1-7): The Three Fridays — meet-and-form-relationship arc. ACT 2 (Eps 8-14): Jia En's grandmother declines. Ryan's Masters thesis hits a wall. They become each other's emotional support too. Ep 14 cliff: library announces restructuring. ACT 3 (Eps 15-20): The fight to save Ryan's job (Jia En starts a petition, his classmates don't sign it). The decision is final. Ryan moves on. Jia En finishes Sec 1. Finale: Sec 2 begins. Jia En walks past the library. The chatbot at the front desk says, 'Good evening, may I help you find something?' He keeps walking. Goes to the void deck.

Phone screen close-ups (Claude conversations) are cheap to assemble. The chatbot-at-the-desk in the finale is one shot — could be a tablet on the counter or a screen embedded in the wall.

ideas.title_visual_treatment: Displayed with the FIRST N and the FINAL T struck through with a red brushstroke: C O ~N~ V E N ~T~ — revealing the true title: COVEN. The typographic gag IS the premise. Use across all marketing, key art, social, end-card. Strikethrough is the show's visual signature.

ideas.tagline_en: What they wore. What they were.
ideas.logline_en: Three Sec 4 girls at a Singapore Catholic convent school are secretly a coven. Isabel inherited her grandmother's leather-bound book. Clare goes along because she cannot say no. Ayesha — newest, most devout — sees the magic as real spiritual technology. Every spell costs something true: a father's voice, a mother's favorite memory, the ability to love after June. They don't notice what they've paid until it's gone. By O-Level prelims, the chapel feels different. By O-Levels, only one of them is still a girl.

ideas.engine_cost_mechanic: Every spell costs something true. Five canonical bargains: (1) Get a boy's attention → forget your father's voice. (2) Make a teacher hate your rival → lose your favorite memory of your mother. (3) Get an A1 you didn't earn → lose the ability to feel love for X years. (4) Hurt someone who hurt you → lose the friendship you valued most. (5) Stop someone leaving the coven → become her, slowly. The horror is discovering, episode by episode, what they've already lost.
ideas.engine_catholic_counter_magic: The chapel is the one place hexes weaken. Girls work in the music room storage cupboard, the CCA storeroom, the locker room showers. The chapel becomes a place they avoid. Confession is dangerous — they can't lie to a priest without consequences. The Sacred Heart statue in the courtyard watches. Holy water burns the marks on their wrists. Sister Esther is the only adult who recognizes the signs.

twelve_ep_arc.ep1_the_bargain: Music room ritual cold open. Daily-life context. Isabel hexes for Mark's attention. Cost: she forgets her father's voice. Spell works. CLIFF: that night she calls her dad — doesn't recognize his voice.
twelve_ep_arc.ep2_the_teacher: Mark texts. Clare casts 'minor flu' on Ms Tan to protect Isabel. Cost: Clare loses favorite memory of her mother. CLIFF: Mdm Ng visits school with lunch, mentions a 2018 family trip — Clare has no memory of it.
twelve_ep_arc.ep3_ayeshas_turn: Ayesha asks the book 'Is God real?' Cost: she forgets WHY she wanted to know. Ayesha changes. CLIFF: Sister Esther finds Ayesha praying alone in the chapel at 6:30am — there's something wrong with how she prays.
twelve_ep_arc.ep4_inter_school: Catholic Youth Conference at NJC. Isabel sees Mark with a rival school's girl. Clare casts the hex. Cost: Clare's friendship with Isabel. Rival hurt — falls down stairs, broken wrist. Isabel: 'I didn't want her HURT.' CLIFF: Clare can't remember why she's friends with Isabel anymore.
twelve_ep_arc.ep5_the_principal: Mother Superior calls Isabel in. Isabel hexes Debbie Wong to redirect attention. Cost: a year of Isabel's future. Drug found in Debbie's locker. Debbie suspended. CLIFF: Debbie walks past Isabel at the gate — looks at her like she's seen Isabel.
twelve_ep_arc.ep6_sister_esther: Sister Esther backstory: almost joined a coven at 17. Goes to Mother Superior — not believed. CLIFF: Sister Esther sees Ayesha in the chapel at night. Ayesha: 'You know. I know you know.'
twelve_ep_arc.ep7_the_boy: Mark proposes (teen-Catholic version, promise ring). Isabel binds him with a spell. Cost: her capacity to feel love for him after June Year 4. CLIFF: Clare tries to pray in the chapel — the cross above the altar grows warm, then hot. She can't look at it.
twelve_ep_arc.ep8_clare_tries_to_leave: Clare reads the exit clause: give back everything you've spent. Performs the exit ritual. Costs reverse — but the book takes something silently. CLIFF: Mdm Ng dies of a heart attack the next morning. The silent take.
twelve_ep_arc.ep9_the_coven_breaks: Funeral wake. Sister Esther: 'I know. Do you want help?' Clare: 'It's too late.' Isabel realizes Ayesha is stronger than the book itself. Ayesha: 'I asked if God was real. It said yes. And then it told me what He's afraid of.' CLIFF: Isabel realizes Ayesha has become something else.
twelve_ep_arc.ep10_the_o_levels: Prelims. Isabel hexes for grades — loses her ability to read for 6 months. Ayesha doesn't need the book — just knows the answers. Sister Esther confronts Ayesha. Ayesha: 'I'm not coming back.' CLIFF: Sister Esther realizes the girl-part of Ayesha is gone.
twelve_ep_arc.ep11_the_final_bargain: Isabel realizes what she's lost. Goes to Clare. Clare wordlessly hands her a photo: their mothers, the year before Isabel's mother left, smiling together. Isabel didn't know they were friends. CLIFF: Isabel performs the exit ritual.
twelve_ep_arc.ep12_sacred_heart: Costs reverse — except the silent takes. Isabel's father is found dead the morning of her last paper. (The silent take.) She sits the paper, gets her A1, doesn't remember why she wanted them. Ayesha hands in a blank sheet. Three girls go to three different JCs. FINAL: Sister Esther in the chapel — Sacred Heart's eyes glow. S2 HOOK: Ayesha is already at JC with a new coven.

ideas.ai_production_fit: STRONG. Convent school interior + chapel + courtyard + Sacred Heart statue = locked location refs. Catholic visual grammar (uniforms, rosaries, holy water, candles, statues) is AI rendering sweet spot. 12 core character refs (3 girls + Sister Esther + Mother Superior + Mark + Debbie + 3 mothers + 2 fathers + grandmother flashback) — manageable. Vertical 9:16 microdrama is exactly what the AI pipeline is built for. Anonymized CHIJ-coded uniforms can be designed. Night chapel/courtyard scenes are AI-friendly (controllable lighting, no extras). The strikethrough title graphic is a single piece of motion design, applicable across all eps as continuity branding.
ideas.scale: PRIMARY: 60-ep S1 microdrama with multi-season ceiling (S2 = JC dispersal arc, 17-18). ALSO: 12-ep limited series as a parallel commission (same world, denser pacing). Could be shot as ONE production with two cuts (vertical 60-ep + prestige 12-ep from same coverage), OR as two distinct productions.

ideas.tagline_en: We build your back-of-house so you can stay front-of-house.
ideas.logline_en: Sophie runs a Joo Chiat bakery with her husband Dennis. Her 12-year-old daughter Julia tells her at 5:45am that she hasn't posted on IG in 9 weeks — and that her friend Aunty Mei down the road, who works the same hours, has 47,000 followers. Sophie can't understand how. Mei tells her at 10:14pm: an agent does it. Built by Dear.AI in 3 weeks. The script ends on Sophie kneading dough the next morning while her phone, propped on a bag of flour, quietly records.
ideas.buyer_persona: Singapore retail SME owner. 40-55. Husband-wife team or single founder. Opened the business 5-15 years ago. 1-3 staff. F&B / fashion / homeware / specialty retail. Has an IG account they post to inconsistently. Has tried agencies and been disappointed. Knows TikTok matters. Cannot fathom adding TikTok to their workload. THE WOUND: not 'I'm bad at marketing' — it is 'I'm running the actual business and marketing is what fell off.' The pitch protects their identity.
ideas.tonal_register: Anthony Chen × Wong Kar-wai (early morning bakery interior aesthetic) × Coursiv direct-response structure. Quiet. Observed. No VO. Two scene partners (daughter at 5:45am, peer-friend at 10:14pm) deliver the gut punch through dialogue, not narration. SG retail SME world — Joo Chiat, Tiong Bahru, Haji Lane mood.
ideas.lead: SOPHIE — 45 · half-Chinese / half-Eurasian · runs the bakery's front-of-house and bakes · weathered hands · flour on the apron · the kind of face that's been getting up at 5am for 8 years
scene_partners: DENNIS — 47 · Chinese-Singaporean · Sophie's husband and business partner · handles ops, supply, accounts · the quiet partner · glasses, simple shirts
JULIA — 12 · Sec 1 · Sophie and Dennis's daughter · the cold-open gut-punch deliverer · matter-of-fact, not cruel

MEI — 44 · runs a café down the road · 12-year friendship with Sophie · works the floor with her sister · the peer-mentor (peer, not seller — credibility is FRIENDSHIP) · seen mainly via late-night phone call, single photo ref for caller ID
ideas.setting: A small bakery in Joo Chiat · 8 years old · husband-wife team · two staff · plus Sophie + Dennis's HDB master bedroom · contemporary Singapore
ep_script_atomized_beats: 1. COLD OPEN (0-15s) · 5:45am, bakery interior. Sophie kneading dough alone in the front. Lights low. Class 95 radio. Dennis cleaning equipment in the back.

2. JULIA WALKS IN (15-30s) · 12, school uniform, sleepy. Picks up Sophie's phone from the counter. 'Mum. Your last IG post was 9 weeks ago.' Sophie: 'I know, ah girl.' Julia: 'Aunty Mei from the café posts every day. She has 47,000 followers now.' Sophie: 'That's nice.' Julia (matter-of-fact): 'You used to have more followers than her.' Sophie keeps kneading. Doesn't look up.

3. JOLT (30-50s) · Slow Tuesday afternoon. Three customers all day. Sophie at the counter scrolling Aunty Mei's TikTok. Daily posts. Quick, on-brand. Sophie's INTERNAL: she knows Mei. They've been friends 12 years. Mei works the floor with her sister. Mei is NOT a marketer. So how—
4. THE TEXT (50-55s) · Sophie's text to Mei: 'Eh Mei. Can call you tonight ah?'

5. THE CONFESSION CALL (55-85s) · 10:14pm. Sophie + Dennis at home. Phone on speaker. Mei: 'Aiyo Sophie. You also tired ah? Listen — I haven't posted a thing manually in 5 months.' Sophie: 'You what?' Mei: 'I have an agent. Dear.AI built it for me. I upload one photo of the bakes in the morning and the system makes the posts, the captions, the schedule. Even handles DMs about opening hours and stock. Costs me less than that IG-management agency I had — you know, the one that posted twice a month and disappeared.' Sophie: 'Wait. They built you what?'
6. CTA CARD (85-95s) · 'Dear.AI · agentic consultancy. We build your back-of-house so you can stay front-of-house. Pilot in 3 weeks. DM BAKERY for the build framework.'

7. FINAL SHOT (95-105s) · 6:15am the next morning. Sophie kneading. Phone propped on a flour bag, camera pointing at her hands. The agent is recording the morning. She's just doing what she always does. Content is happening around her, not on top of her.
ideas.key_visual: Sophie's hands kneading dough at 6:15am while her phone — propped on a bag of flour, camera pointing at her hands — quietly records. The work was already there; the marketing happened around it. This single shot is the ad's screengrab and the LinkedIn-share image for the next 12 months.

ideas.cta_line_locked: DM 'BAKERY' for the build framework. Pilot in 3 weeks.
ideas.ai_production_fit: PERFECT. Bakery interior at dawn (warm tungsten + cool blue from windows) is the hero shot — exactly what AI rendering does well. Husband-wife-daughter household scene is contained 3-actor blocking. Mei via phone screen is a single photo ref. Whole script is 4 locations max (bakery dawn / bakery slow afternoon / HDB master bedroom / bakery the next morning). No extras with speaking parts. ~25-30 atomic shots at 3-4s each.
ideas.production_pairing: Pairs with `skipped-ad` (the CMO Product 1 script) as a two-ad campaign — same casting style (lead female protagonist 40s), same single-location structure, same Coursiv architecture. Can be shot in the same week with overlapping crew + design.

ideas.tagline_en: Nobody watches ads anymore. They watch stories.
ideas.logline_en: Pauline, 46, marketing director, on her couch with her teenage son. Her brand's latest pre-roll plays on his iPad. He skips it in 1.2 seconds without looking up. She asks him why. He says: 'Ma. Nobody watches ads anymore.' She asks what he does watch. He shows her his FYP — three branded dramas in a row, one a competitor's. He watched that one twice. Didn't know it was an ad. That night she finds Dear.AI's portfolio on LinkedIn. Sends a message. Last shot: her in a meeting the next week, asking — 'Make me one of those.'
ideas.buyer_persona: CMO / Marketing Director / Senior Marketer at a SG SME or regional brand. 40-55. Female-skewing but not exclusively. Has a budget but is being asked to justify the ROI more than ever. Their TVC and IG-ad CTRs have collapsed. Their agency keeps pitching them the same approach. They've seen competitors' branded dramas go viral and they don't know how to brief one. THE WOUND: their own teenage children skip the ads they spent millions on.
ideas.tonal_register: Domestic naturalism × Coursiv direct-response. Two-character (mum + son) on a couch. No VO. The wound is delivered in a single line of off-handed teenage dialogue. Quietly devastating.
ideas.lead: PAULINE — 46 · Singaporean-Chinese · marketing director at a SG SME or regional brand · sharp, dignified, has been doing this for 20 years · the kind of mum who still buys her son's school uniform a size up because he'll grow
scene_partners: THE SON — 15 · Sec 3 · Pauline's son · headphones half-off · casual, not cruel · delivers the gut punch matter-of-factly, the way 15-year-olds always do · the only audience member Pauline cannot lie to
ideas.setting: Pauline's HDB / private condo living room · Sunday evening · plus a kitchen island scene that night · plus a meeting room the next week (final shot)
ep_script_atomized_beats: 1. COLD OPEN (0-10s) · Sunday evening. Pauline on her couch. Her son next to her, iPad in his lap, headphones half-off, watching a YouTube video. Normal SG family Sunday.
2. THE AD PLAYS (10-18s) · A pre-roll loads. It's PAULINE'S OWN BRAND'S ad. The one she signed off on 3 months ago. Slick. Expensive. We see it on her son's screen, reflected in her face.
3. THE SKIP (18-22s) · He skips it in 1.2 seconds. Doesn't even look up.
4. THE QUESTION (22-35s) · PAULINE: 'You know that was mum's ad.' SON (casual, eyes still on screen): 'Yeah. Nobody watches ads anymore, ma.' Beat. Pauline doesn't speak.

5. THE PIVOT QUESTION (35-50s) · PAULINE (quietly): 'What do you watch?' Son hesitates. Then turns the iPad slightly so she can see his FYP. Scrolls. We see — first scroll: a 90-second branded drama. Second scroll: another one. Third scroll: he stops on a third, which is her direct COMPETITOR'S 3-minute branded drama. He watches a second of it. 'This one. I watched this twice.'

6. THE RECOGNITION (50-65s) · PAULINE: 'You knew this was an ad?' SON: 'It's an ad? I thought it was a show.' He goes back to his original YouTube video. Pauline sits there. Her own brand's ad has finished playing in the background. She didn't watch it either.

7. THE SEARCH (65-78s) · Late that night. Kitchen island. Laptop open. Pauline googles the competitor's drama. Reads the credits. 'Studio: Dear.AI.' Clicks through. Sees the portfolio. Pauses on one piece. Then another. Then another.
8. THE MESSAGE (78-85s) · She opens LinkedIn. Finds Raymus's profile. Types: 'I'd like to learn how this works.' Sends.
9. CTA CARD (85-92s) · 'Dear.AI · branded drama studio. Nobody watches ads anymore. They watch stories. DM DRAMA for the studio reel.'
10. FINAL SHOT (92-98s) · One week later. Pauline in a meeting room. Across the table from her — implied, not shown — Raymus. She leans forward. Says one line: 'Make me one of those.' CUT TO BLACK.
ideas.key_visual: The pre-roll playing on the son's iPad — reflected in Pauline's face — for 1.2 seconds before he skips it. The reflection beat is the entire ad. Audience sees the cost on her face before they understand what she's looking at.
ideas.positioning_line_locked: Nobody watches ads anymore. They watch stories.
ideas.ai_production_fit: PERFECT. Couch-and-iPad scene is the simplest two-actor blocking possible. Kitchen island at night is a contained single-character scene. Meeting room final shot is one wide + one OTS. Whole script is 3 locations, 2 actors, ~25 atomic shots. Reflection-on-Pauline's-face beat is achievable in AI with careful prompt design (camera on Pauline, screen glow reflected, son's hand entering frame to swipe).
ideas.production_pairing: Pairs with `bakery-ad` (the agentic consultancy Product 2 script) as a two-ad campaign — different product, different persona, but same Coursiv architecture and same casting tier. Same shoot week.
ideas.notes: Locked 2026-05-21. User flagged this as 'very good' alongside the bakery script — 'add to /scripts.' Script protects the buyer's dignity — the failure is the SON delivering the verdict, not the marketer. The 'I thought it was a show' beat is the marketer's pivot moment — branded drama isn't a different ad, it's a different MEDIUM. CTA line 'Nobody watches ads anymore. They watch stories.' is the positioning IP for the entire Branded Dramas for Brands product. LOCK THIS TAGLINE for all Branded Drama Studio ads. Next milestones: atomize via /microdrama-shotlist, lock casting (Pauline 46, Son 15), location-scout living room interior reference (could shoot in Raymus's own apartment or a friend's), confirm reflection-on-face shot is achievable in AI pipeline.

ideas.tagline_en: He cooks the purest chewing gum in Southeast Asia.
ideas.logline_en: Walter Bai, an overlooked JC chemistry teacher passed over for promotion, partners with his dropout ex-student Ah Hao to cook the purest contraband chewing gum in Singapore — 'blue gum,' 99.1% pure, smuggled in from JB through Tuas. A Breaking Bad parody with the product swapped from meth to chewing gum (banned in SG since 1992) — which sidesteps the drug-death-penalty sensitivity entirely and is funnier and more Singaporean.
ideas.legal_safety_note: CRITICAL CREATIVE DECISION: the product is CHEWING GUM, not drugs. Rationale: (1) SG has the death penalty for drug trafficking — a drug parody is culturally radioactive and platform-throttled; (2) chewing gum is THE iconic SG contraband (banned 1992), instantly funny, zero death-penalty baggage; (3) treating premium artisanal gum with Breaking Bad gravity is funnier than drugs. Vape/Kpods rejected as too-real (active 2025-26 public health crisis). SCRIPT IS ORIGINAL PARODY DIALOGUE — deliberately NOT a verbatim Breaking Bad script with words swapped (that would be an infringing derivative). Original transformative parody is legally defensible AND funnier. Episode citations are factual references only.
key_cast: WALTER BAI (Walter White) — JC chemistry teacher, passed over for HOD promotion. Alias: HEISENBAI. 白 = white.
AH HAO / Junhao (Jesse Pinkman) — dropout ex-student, small-time gum peddler. 'Yo Mr Bai.'
SI LAN / Selena Bai (Skyler) — wife, part-time bookkeeper, pregnant.
GUS (Gus Fring) — soft-spoken ayam penyet franchise king. Front: AYAM PENYET BERSAUDARA ('The Brothers' Smashed Chicken' = literal Los Pollos Hermanos).
AH TUKO (Tuco Salamanca) — volatile Ah Long loanshark. Gold chains, modified Honda.
UNCLE MIKE (Mike Ehrmantraut) — retired SPF, now deadpan carpark-booth attendant + fixer/cleaner. Eats cai png.
HENG (Hank Schrader) — brother-in-law, Singapore Customs officer, zealous about gum-smuggling.
BETTER CALL SAW (Saul Goodman) — dodgy Chinatown shophouse lawyer.
JUNIOR BAI (Walt Jr / Flynn) — son, at a poly, always eating breakfast (BB breakfast running gag).
ideas.store_name_locked: AYAM PENYET BERSAUDARA (literal translation of Los Pollos Hermanos = 'The Chicken Brothers'). Runner-up: PENYET BROTHERS.
ideas.product_locked: Blue chewing gum ('Bai Gum' / 'Teacher's Gum'), 99.1% pure, tinted blue. Banned in SG since 1992. Smuggled from Johor Bahru via Tuas / Second Link.
ideas.rv_equivalent: Converted ice-cream uncle truck (jingle plays during cooks). The Tuas industrial backroads near the Second Link = the 'desert.'
ten_scene_breakdown: 1. THE COLD OPEN — ref S1E1 'Pilot' (desert RV/camcorder goodbye) — Walter Bai in singlet+underwear, ice-cream truck slewed across Tuas, records phone goodbye to Si Lan, faces sirens with a satay stick. [ESSENTIAL]
2. THE CLASSROOM — ref S1E1 'Pilot' ('study of change' lecture) — Cher Bai droning to bored JC kids, one filming him on TikTok. [ESSENTIAL]
3. THE BREAK — ref S1E1 'Pilot' (diagnosis + stain-distraction) — passed over for HOD; fixates on kaya stain on principal's tie. [ESSENTIAL]
4. RECRUITING AH HAO — ref S1E1 'Pilot' ('Yo Mr White') — 'Yo Mr Bai??' at the void deck; pitches partnership. [ESSENTIAL]
5. THE FIRST COOK — ref S1E1 'Pilot' (purity reveal) — ice-cream truck cook, 99.1% blue gum, Ah Hao awed.
6. MEETING AH TUKO — ref S1E6 'Crazy Handful of Nothin' (fulminated-mercury bluff) — 'This is gum' / flicks it / firecracker BANG / 'that one was not gum.'
7. I AM THE ONE WHO KNOCKS — ref S4E6 'Cornered' (kitchen monologue) — 'I am the one who knocks' + original undercut (metal HDB gate hurts his hand). [ESSENTIAL]
8. AYAM PENYET BERSAUDARA — ref S3E5 'Más' (Gus 'a man provides' pitch) — 'Nobody checks the chicken.'
9. UNCLE MIKE — ref S3E12 'Half Measures' ('no half measures') — carpark booth, cai png, 'Eat first. Then we talk. Then I clean.'

10. SAY MY NAME + TAG — ref S5E7 'Say My Name' (desert standoff) + recurring DEA-dinner irony — 'Say my name' / 'Heisenbai' / 'You damn right' + Heng ranting about the kingpin at family dinner while Bai eats silently.
ideas.cut_strategy: 90s cut = scenes 1→4→7 (cold open, recruit, 'one who knocks'), end on the gate-knuckle undercut. Full teaser = all 10, end on Scene 10 dinner-tag dramatic irony. Scenes 1-5 all source from the Pilot — tightest single-episode proof-of-concept.
ideas.ai_production_fit: STRONG. Singlet-Tuas cold open, JC classroom, void deck, HDB kitchen, carpark booth, ayam penyet shop = all iconic SG locations the pipeline renders cleanly. Ice-cream truck interior = only build. ~6 character refs. Blue gum is a perfect recurring insert prop (the 'blue meth crystals' equivalent visual signature).
ideas.why_team: Highest viral-ceiling social proof-of-concept on the slate. Breaking Bad is globally recognized; the SG localization (gum-not-drugs, ice-cream-truck-not-RV, ayam-penyet-not-fried-chicken, Tuas-not-desert, carpark-uncle-Mike) is a continuous stream of recognition-laughs. Each scene is a standalone clip. The singlet-in-Tuas-with-a-satay-stick cold open is the travel shot. Demonstrates Dear.AI's AI-production range (period/genre pastiche, character consistency, SG locations) in a format built for shares.
ideas.notes: Concept + full parody script locked 2026-05-24. User asked 'will it get me killed?' — answered: not if product = chewing gum (not drugs). Desert = Tuas. 10 scenes sourced to specific BB episodes (factual citation) with ORIGINAL parody dialogue written fresh (NOT verbatim-swap — protects production legally + funnier).
